essentials.heading: Instance families & sizing
essentials.body: EC2 families map to a workload profile. **General purpose** (`t`, `m`) balance CPU/memory; `t` types are *burstable* (CPU credits) for spiky low-baseline workloads. **Compute optimized** (`c`) suit batch, gaming, HPC, and media transcoding (high CPU\:memory). **Memory optimized** (`r`, `x`, `z`) fit in-memory caches and large relational/NoSQL DBs. **Storage optimized** (`i`, `d`, `h`) deliver high local NVMe IOPS for data warehouses and NoSQL. **Accelerated** (`p`, `g`, `inf`) add GPUs/ML chips. Naming = `family + generation + size`, e.g. `m6g.large` (`g` suffix = Graviton ARM). Right-size by matching the bottleneck resource, not by always going bigger.

essentials.heading: Purchasing options
essentials.body: **On-Demand**: pay per second/hour, no commitment — best for short, unpredictable, dev/test workloads. **Reserved Instances (RIs)**: 1 or 3-year commitment to a specific family/region for up to ~72% savings; *Standard* RIs save most but are rigid, *Convertible* RIs allow changing attributes. **Savings Plans**: commit to a $/hour spend for 1-3 years — *Compute SP* is most flexible (any region/family/even Fargate/Lambda), *EC2 Instance SP* is cheaper but family+region locked. **Spot**: up to ~90% off spare capacity but can be reclaimed with a 2-minute warning — ideal for *fault-tolerant, stateless, batch, CI, big-data* workloads, never for stateful single-node databases. **Dedicated Hosts/Instances** address licensing and compliance isolation.

essentials.heading: AMIs, user data & instance metadata
essentials.body: An **AMI** is the launch template image (OS + pre-installed software + block device mapping). Bake dependencies into a *custom/golden AMI* to cut boot time vs. installing at launch. **User data** is a script run *once at first boot* (cloud-init) for bootstrapping — passed base64-encoded, runs as root. **Instance metadata service (IMDS)** is reached at `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` for instance facts and IAM role temp credentials. Prefer **IMDSv2** (session/token-based, `PUT` then `GET`) to defend against SSRF; IMDSv1 is the older request/response model.

essentials.heading: Placement groups
essentials.body: Control how instances are physically placed. **Cluster**: packs instances in one AZ on the same rack for lowest latency / highest throughput (HPC) — but a rack failure can take them all down. **Spread**: places each instance on distinct hardware (max 7 per AZ per group) for critical instances needing isolation. **Partition**: groups instances into partitions on separate racks (up to 7 partitions/AZ), so one partition failure does not affect others — used by large distributed/replicated systems like HDFS, Cassandra, Kafka.

essentials.heading: Elastic Load Balancing — ALB vs NLB vs GWLB
essentials.body: **ALB** (Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS): host-based and path-based routing, query-string/header rules, redirects, WebSockets, native to containers (dynamic port mapping) — but no static IP (use DNS name). **NLB** (Layer 4, TCP/UDP/TLS): ultra-low latency, millions of req/s, and one **static IP / Elastic IP per AZ** — choose it when you need a fixed IP or extreme performance. **Gateway Load Balancer** (Layer 3/4, GENEVE port 6081): transparently inserts third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS, deep packet inspection) into the traffic path. All ELBs span multiple AZs for HA and perform health checks before routing.

essentials.heading: Auto Scaling Groups
essentials.body: An **ASG** maintains desired/min/max capacity across multiple AZs and replaces unhealthy instances. Define capacity with a **launch template** (preferred over legacy launch configs; supports versions and mixed instances/Spot). **Scaling policies**: *Target tracking* keeps a metric at a target (e.g. avg CPU 50%) and is the simplest; *Step/simple scaling* adjusts by CloudWatch alarm thresholds; *Scheduled scaling* for predictable time-based load. **Health checks** can use EC2 status or **ELB health checks** (recommended so app-level failures trigger replacement). **Cooldown** (simple scaling) and warm-up periods prevent flapping by pausing further scaling until metrics stabilize.

essentials.heading: Connection draining / deregistration delay
essentials.body: When an instance is being deregistered or marked unhealthy, **deregistration delay** (ALB/NLB term; *connection draining* for Classic LB) lets in-flight requests finish before the target is removed. Default is **300 seconds**, configurable 0-3600. The target enters a `draining` state: no new connections, existing ones complete or are cut at timeout. Set it low for short requests to scale in faster; set it higher for long-lived connections (uploads, streaming) to avoid dropping users mid-request.

labs.title: Launch an EC2 from a launch template with user data
labs.intro: Create a **launch template** that bootstraps a web server via *user data*, then launch one instance from it. Bootstrapping at first boot avoids hand-configuring servers.
labs.cli:
# Base64-encode user data that installs and starts nginx
USER_DATA=$(printf '#!/bin/bash\ndnf install -y nginx\nsystemctl enable --now nginx\necho "hello from $(hostname -f)" > /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html\n' | base64 -w0)

aws ec2 create-launch-template \
  --launch-template-name web-lt \
  --version-description v1 \
  --launch-template-data '{
    "ImageId": "ami-0abcdef1234567890",
    "InstanceType": "t3.micro",
    "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-0123456789abcdef0"],
    "MetadataOptions": {"HttpTokens": "required"},
    "UserData": "'"$USER_DATA"'"
  }'

aws ec2 run-instances \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateName=web-lt,Version=1 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=lab-web}]'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi
import pulumi_aws as aws
import base64

user_data = base64.b64encode(
    b"#!/bin/bash\n"
    b"dnf install -y nginx\n"
    b"systemctl enable --now nginx\n"
).decode()

lt = aws.ec2.LaunchTemplate(
    "web-lt",
    name="web-lt",
    image_id="ami-0abcdef1234567890",
    instance_type="t3.micro",
    vpc_security_group_ids=["sg-0123456789abcdef0"],
    user_data=user_data,
    metadata_options=aws.ec2.LaunchTemplateMetadataOptionsArgs(
        http_tokens="required",  # IMDSv2
    ),
)

instance = aws.ec2.Instance(
    "lab-web",
    launch_template=aws.ec2.InstanceLaunchTemplateArgs(
        id=lt.id, version="$Latest"
    ),
    tags={"Name": "lab-web"},
)
pulumi.export("instance_id", instance.id)
labs.note: **Cleanup**: `aws ec2 terminate-instances --instance-ids <id>` then `aws ec2 delete-launch-template --launch-template-name web-lt`. With Pulumi just `pulumi destroy`. Replace the placeholder AMI ID with a current one from `aws ssm get-parameter --name /aws/service/ami-amazon-linux-latest/al2023-ami-kernel-default-x86_64`.

labs.title: ASG behind an ALB across two AZs
labs.intro: Build a resilient tier: an **ALB** spanning 2 subnets/AZs forwarding to a **target group**, fed by an **Auto Scaling Group** using the launch template, with *target-tracking* on CPU.
labs.cli:
# Target group + ALB (assumes 2 public subnets in different AZs)
TG_ARN=$(aws elbv2 create-target-group --name web-tg --protocol HTTP --port 80 \
  --vpc-id vpc-0123456789abcdef0 --target-type instance \
  --health-check-path / --query 'TargetGroups[0].TargetGroupArn' --output text)

LB_ARN=$(aws elbv2 create-load-balancer --name web-alb --type application \
  --subnets subnet-aaa subnet-bbb --security-groups sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --query 'LoadBalancers[0].LoadBalancerArn' --output text)

aws elbv2 create-listener --load-balancer-arn $LB_ARN --protocol HTTP --port 80 \
  --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=$TG_ARN

# ASG across both AZs, ELB health checks, target tracking on CPU
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateName=web-lt,Version='$Latest' \
  --min-size 2 --max-size 4 --desired-capacity 2 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier 'subnet-aaa,subnet-bbb' \
  --target-group-arns $TG_ARN --health-check-type ELB --health-check-grace-period 60

aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --policy-name cpu-tt --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-configuration '{"PredefinedMetricSpecification":{"PredefinedMetricType":"ASGAverageCPUUtilization"},"TargetValue":50.0}'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

tg = aws.lb.TargetGroup(
    "web-tg", port=80, protocol="HTTP", target_type="instance",
    vpc_id="vpc-0123456789abcdef0",
    health_check=aws.lb.TargetGroupHealthCheckArgs(path="/"),
)
alb = aws.lb.LoadBalancer(
    "web-alb", load_balancer_type="application",
    subnets=["subnet-aaa", "subnet-bbb"],
    security_groups=["sg-0123456789abcdef0"],
)
listener = aws.lb.Listener(
    "http", load_balancer_arn=alb.arn, port=80, protocol="HTTP",
    default_actions=[aws.lb.ListenerDefaultActionArgs(
        type="forward", target_group_arn=tg.arn)],
)
asg = aws.autoscaling.Group(
    "web-asg", min_size=2, max_size=4, desired_capacity=2,
    vpc_zone_identifiers=["subnet-aaa", "subnet-bbb"],
    target_group_arns=[tg.arn],
    health_check_type="ELB", health_check_grace_period=60,
    launch_template=aws.autoscaling.GroupLaunchTemplateArgs(
        name="web-lt", version="$Latest"),
)
aws.autoscaling.Policy(
    "cpu-tt", autoscaling_group_name=asg.name,
    policy_type="TargetTrackingScaling",
    target_tracking_configuration=aws.autoscaling.PolicyTargetTrackingConfigurationArgs(
        predefined_metric_specification=aws.autoscaling.PolicyTargetTrackingConfigurationPredefinedMetricSpecificationArgs(
            predefined_metric_type="ASGAverageCPUUtilization"),
        target_value=50.0,
    ),
)
labs.note: **Cleanup order matters**: delete the ASG first (`aws autoscaling delete-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg --force-delete`), then the listener, ALB, and target group. `pulumi destroy` handles ordering automatically. Spanning 2 AZs is the core resilience pattern — if one AZ fails the ASG keeps `min-size` healthy in the other.

questions.stem: A web application must stay available even if an entire Availability Zone goes down. Which architecture best meets this requirement?
options: A single large EC2 instance with an Elastic IP and detailed monitoring
An Auto Scaling Group with min size 2 spread across two AZs behind an Application Load Balancer
Two EC2 instances in the same AZ in a cluster placement group behind an ALB
A single instance with an EBS snapshot lifecycle policy for fast recovery
questions.explanation: Correct: an ASG spanning two AZs behind an ALB keeps healthy capacity in the surviving AZ and auto-replaces failed instances — this is the canonical multi-AZ resilience pattern. A single instance (options A and D) is a single point of failure regardless of EIP or snapshots. A cluster placement group keeps instances in one AZ for low latency, so an AZ failure takes them all down.

questions.stem: A nightly batch job processes data for 3 hours, can be interrupted and restarted safely, and must minimize cost. Which purchasing option is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Correct: Spot offers up to ~90% savings and is ideal for fault-tolerant, interruptible batch work — exactly this profile. Reserved Instances require a long commitment and suit steady 24/7 usage, wasteful for a 3-hour nightly job. On-Demand is far more expensive than Spot for interruptible work. Dedicated Hosts address licensing/compliance isolation, not cost minimization.

questions.stem: An application needs a load balancer that provides a static IP address and ultra-low latency for millions of TCP connections per second. Which option fits best?
questions.explanation: Correct: the NLB operates at Layer 4, handles millions of req/s at ultra-low latency, and provides a static IP / Elastic IP per AZ. The ALB is Layer 7 (HTTP) with no static IP. The Gateway Load Balancer is for inserting third-party network appliances. The Classic Load Balancer is legacy and not recommended for new designs.

questions.stem: A single application must route requests to different target groups based on the URL path (/api vs /images). Which load balancer feature provides this?
questions.explanation: Correct: path-based (and host-based) routing is a Layer 7 ALB feature, letting listener rules forward by URL path to different target groups. The NLB is Layer 4 and cannot inspect URLs. GWLB inserts appliances and does not do app routing. Route 53 weighted records split DNS traffic by percentage, not by URL path.

questions.stem: An in-memory analytics workload needs the highest possible RAM-to-vCPU ratio at the lowest cost for that profile. Which instance family should you choose?
questions.explanation: Correct: memory-optimized (R, X) families give the highest memory-to-vCPU ratio for in-memory databases and analytics. Compute-optimized (C) favors CPU, not RAM. Storage-optimized (I) targets high local NVMe IOPS. Burstable T instances have low baseline CPU and modest memory — unsuitable for sustained memory-heavy work.

questions.stem: A distributed HDFS-style cluster must tolerate the failure of underlying hardware without losing more than one replica group, while still benefiting from grouped placement. Which placement group type fits?
questions.explanation: Correct: partition placement groups split instances into partitions on separate racks, so a hardware failure affects only one partition — the design pattern for HDFS, Cassandra, and Kafka. A cluster group packs everything on one rack (shared failure domain). A spread group caps at 7 instances per AZ, too small for large clusters. Default placement gives no rack-isolation guarantees.

questions.stem: Users report dropped uploads whenever the Auto Scaling Group scales in. The application has long-running connections. Which two changes reduce dropped requests? (Choose two)
options: Enable instance scale-in protection or use a longer cooldown to avoid premature termination
Switch the ASG health check type from ELB to EC2
Increase the target group deregistration delay (connection draining)
Replace the ALB with a Classic Load Balancer
questions.explanation: Correct: raising the deregistration delay lets in-flight long-lived connections finish before the target is removed, and scale-in protection / longer cooldowns prevent terminating instances that still serve traffic. Switching ELB health checks to EC2 is wrong — it removes app-level failure detection and does nothing for draining. Replacing the ALB with a Classic LB does not address draining behavior and is a legacy step backward.

questions.stem: A company has a steady, predictable baseline of compute running 24/7 across several instance families and regions, plus some Fargate usage. They want the deepest savings with maximum flexibility. Which commitment best fits?
options: Standard Reserved Instances locked to one instance type and region
Spot Instances with a maximum price
questions.explanation: Correct: a Compute Savings Plan commits to a $/hour spend and applies across any instance family, size, region, and even Fargate/Lambda — best for steady usage needing flexibility. Standard RIs save well but are locked to a type/region, lacking the requested flexibility. Spot is for interruptible work, not steady 24/7 baselines. On-Demand Capacity Reservations guarantee capacity but provide no discount.
